sections.summary: Applies to commercial varieties of oranges (Citrus sinensis) supplied fresh to consumers after preparation and packaging; oranges intended for industrial processing are excluded.

Sets provisions concerning quality including minimum requirements, maturity criteria (colouring and minimum juice content) and classification into three classes with specified allowed defects and tolerances.

Specifies general quality requirements for oranges (whole, sound, clean, practically free of pests and visible defects, free of abnormal moisture, foreign odours, temperature damage, frost damage and internal shrivelling) and requires appropriate development and ripeness to withstand transport; degreening is permitted only if other natural organoleptic characteristics are not modified.

Specifies general quality requirements for oranges (whole, sound, clean, practically free of pests and visible defects, free of abnormal moisture, foreign odours, temperature damage, frost damage and internal shrivelling) and requires appropriate development and ripeness to withstand transport; degreening is permitted only if other natural organoleptic characteristics are not modified.

sections.section: 2.1.1  The  oranges  must  have  reached  an  appropriate  degree  of  development  and  ripeness  account  being taken of the characteristics of the variety, the time of picking and the area in which they are grown.
sections.summary: Oranges must have reached an appropriate degree of development and ripeness (considering variety, picking time and growing area) such that they can withstand transport and arrive in satisfactory condition; degreening is allowed only if it does not alter other natural organoleptic characteristics.
Defines maturity criteria for oranges based on colouring and minimum juice content (juice measured as percentage of total fruit weight after hand‑press extraction).

Colouring must be typical of the variety and reach normal variety colour at destination; fruits with light green colour are allowed up to one‑fifth of surface area, and fruit from hot, humid areas may exceed one‑fifth green provided they meet the juice criteria in Section 2.2.2.
Specifies minimum juice content by variety/group: Blood oranges 30%, Navels group 33%, other varieties 35%, Mosambi/Sathgudi/Pacitan with >1/5 green 33%, and other varieties with >1/5 green 45%.
Oranges are classified into three classes ('Extra', Class I and Class II), with each class defined by quality criteria and permitted defects.

Defines 'Extra' Class oranges as superior quality fruit, characteristic of the variety/commercial type and free of defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation in the package.

Class I oranges must be of good quality and characteristic of the variety/commercial type; slight defects (shape, colouring, certain skin scuffs or healed mechanical damage) are permitted provided they do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation, and in no case may defects affect the pulp.

Class II covers oranges that meet the minimum requirements but not higher classes and may show defects (shape, colouring, formation skin defects, healed mechanical damage, rough or superficially altered skin, or slight partial detachment of the pericarp) provided the fruit retain essential quality, keeping quality and presentation, and defects must not affect the pulp.

Size is defined by maximum equatorial diameter with a size-code table and fruit under 53 mm excluded; packing by count may span two adjacent size codes if uniformity is retained, and specific maximum size-difference limits are set for fruit arranged in layers, not in layers/rigid consumer packages, and for bulk/non-rigid consumer packages (grouping three consecutive sizes).

Allows tolerances in each package for produce not meeting the indicated class: quality tolerances are detailed by class (Extra 5%, Class I 10%, Class II 10% with exclusions and sublimits), and size tolerances permit 10% by number or weight of fruit one size above/below the labeled size, applying only to fruit with diameter ≥50 mm.
Section heading only (4.1 Quality Tolerances) with no substantive provisions in this entry.
Permits up to 5% by number or weight of 'Extra' Class oranges that do not meet the class requirements but meet Class I, or exceptionally fall within Class I tolerances.
Permits up to 10% by number or weight of Class I oranges that do not meet Class I requirements but meet Class II, or exceptionally fall within Class II tolerances.

Permits up to 10% by number or weight of Class II oranges that satisfy neither the class nor the minimum requirements (excluding produce affected by rotting or other deterioration making it unfit), and within this tolerance a maximum of 5% may show slight superficial unhealed damage, dry cuts, or be soft/shrivelled.

sections.section: Within  this  tolerance,  a  maximum  of  5%  is  allowed  of  fruit  showing  slight  superficial  unhealed damage, dry cuts or soft and shrivelled fruit.
sections.summary: Specifies that, within the Class II tolerance, a maximum of 5% may consist of fruit showing slight superficial unhealed damage, dry cuts, or soft and shrivelled fruit.

For all classes, allows 10% by number or weight of fruit to be the size immediately above and/or below the labeled size, with this 10% size tolerance applying only to fruit of diameter not less than 50 mm.
Section heading only (5. Provisions Concerning Presentation) with no substantive provisions in this entry.

Requires package contents to be uniform in origin, variety/commercial type, quality, size and appreciably the same ripeness/development, with the visible contents representative of the whole, and additionally requires uniform colouring for 'Extra' Class.

Oranges must be packed to protect the produce using new, clean materials (including food-grade recycled material) that do not cause damage, with non-toxic inks/glues allowed for trade printing, and packing must comply with the Recommended International Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CAC/RCP 44-1995).
Containers must meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance requirements to ensure proper handling, shipping and preservation of oranges, and packages must be free of foreign matter and odours.

Specifies permitted presentation forms: (a) arranged in regular layers (mandatory for 'Extra', optional for Classes I and II); (b) not arranged in packages (only allowed for Classes I and II); and (c) individual consumer packages under 5 kg, by number or weight.
Section heading only (6. Marking or Labelling) with no substantive provisions in this entry.
States that, in addition to the Codex General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CODEX STAN 1-1985), specific additional labelling provisions for consumer packages apply.
If the produce is not visible from outside, each consumer package or bulk lot must be labelled with the name of the produce and may also be labelled with the variety and/or commercial type.
Non-retail containers must bear legible, indelible particulars grouped on the same side and visible from outside, or the required particulars must accompany the shipment in the documents.
Requires labelling to include the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher; an identification code may be optionally provided.
Where contents are not visible, the label must state the name of the produce; the variety and/or commercial type may be declared optionally.
Labelling must declare the country of origin and may optionally include the district or a national, regional or local place name of where the produce was grown.

Commercial identification on the package must include class and appropriate size information: a size code per the size scale (or upper/lower limiting codes for three consecutive sizes), and for fruit packed by count the size code(s) or minimum/maximum diameter in mm plus the number of fruit for layered packages.
Non-substantive heading indicating an optional official inspection mark; no details provided in this section.

States that the produce must comply with the Codex General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CODEX STAN 193-1995) and with Codex-established maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Recommends preparation and handling in accordance with Codex food hygiene codes (General Principles, Code for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables and other relevant texts) and requires compliance with any microbiological criteria established under CAC/GL 21-1997.
